paper:966036046494040104.zh_title: 纯碳的苏曼烯单层：一种具有理想带隙、超高载流子迁移率和强激子结合能的二维类 Kagome 晶格

variables.content:
At each density, spin polarization, and finite system size the discrete DMC band values \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) (where \\(k=|\\mathbf{k}|\\)) were fitted over an extended wavevector interval to a quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4},
      $$
      with fitted coefficients \\(\\alpha_{0},\\alpha_{2},\\alpha_{4}\\). The quasiparticle effective mass was then obtained from the definition
      $$
      m^{*}=\\frac{k_{F}}{\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}},
      $$
      where \\(k_{F}\\) is the Fermi wavevector of the infinite 2D HEG at the given density and spin polarization and the derivative is evaluated at \\(k=k_{F}\\). It was observed that extracting the derivative from fits restricted to a very narrow interval immediately surrounding \\(k_{F}\\) yields ill-behaved (pathological) estimates of \\(m^{*}\\), whereas performing the quartic fit over a larger modest wavevector range around \\(k_{F}\\) produces stable, systematically varying estimates of \\(m^{*}\\). Averaging the band behaviour over such a modest \\(k\\)-range therefore provides a controlled practical procedure to suppress the finite-cell and Hartree–Fock-like near-\\(k_{F}\\) fluctuations that cause the pathological behaviour.

Assume the true thermodynamic-limit single-quasiparticle band of a 2D HEG is a smooth function \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) in a neighbourhood of the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: fitting the finite-cell discrete DMC band values to the quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4}
      $$
      over a modest interval \\([k_{F}-\\Delta k,\\,k_{F}+\\Delta k]\\) (with \\(\\Delta k\\) chosen so that the interval is not extremely small and contains sufficient discrete \\(k\\)-points to characterise curvature) yields an estimate of the derivative \\(\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}\\) that is an adequately accurate approximation to the true thermodynamic-limit derivative for the purpose of computing the quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}=k_{F}/(d\\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{F}}\\), provided the quartic form captures the local curvature of \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\).

To obtain the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass from finite-N DMC band data one may (i) fit the computed isotropic band values E(k), with k = |𝗸|, in a finite interval k∈[k_F−Δk,k_F+Δk] about the Fermi wave vector k_F to the quartic polynomial E(k)=α_0+α_2 k^2+α_4 k^4 (with fit coefficients α_0, α_2, α_4), (ii) compute the band derivative at k_F from the fit as (dE/dk)_{k_F}=2α_2 k_F+4α_4 k_F^3 and define the quasiparticle effective mass by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}, and (iii) evaluate m^*(N) for several simulation-cell electron counts N and fit the N dependence to the single power-law model m^*(N)=m^*(∞)+b N^{-γ} (with fit parameters m^*(∞), b, and γ) to obtain the thermodynamic-limit mass m^*(∞); when the quartic fit in step (i) is performed over a sufficiently wide Δk about k_F (so as not to be dominated by pathological near-k_F fluctuations) this three-step procedure yields stable extrapolated values m^*(∞).

For the interacting 2D HEG energy-band data sets produced by DMC at the densities ($r_s=1,5,10$) and system sizes reported, truncating the band representation to a quartic polynomial in $k$, $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$, captures the essential curvature: the least-squares fits yield residuals that are small compared with the quoted uncertainties, so that the fitted coefficients $\alpha_2$ and $\alpha_4$ and derived quantities (occupied bandwidth and derivative at $k_F$ used to obtain $m^{*}$) are accurate within the stated error bars and higher-order terms ($k^6$ and beyond) are negligible over the occupied $k$-range used for the reported extractions.

In finite periodic simulation cells using Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions (where the Jastrow and backflow factors each contain variational parameters), reoptimizing the Jastrow and backflow parameters for an excited-state configuration (an excitation produced by adding or removing an electron in a particular Bloch state) can lower the total DMC energy in that finite cell—for example, removing an electron from 𝗸=0 in a 26-electron paramagnetic 2D HEG at density parameter r_s=1 reduced the DMC total energy by 0.000854(4) hartree atomic units (a.u.)—but this finite-cell energy lowering is a manifestation of finite-size error and tends to reduce the computed excitation bandwidth and thus amplify finite-size bias in bandwidth-derived quantities such as the quasiparticle effective mass; therefore, to avoid introducing extra finite-size bias the Jastrow and backflow parameters are optimized in the ground state and then reused unchanged for excited-state energy evaluations.

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

For each relevant band extremum at crystal momentum k_0 ∈ {Γ, K}, fitting the DFT band dispersion ε_n(k) to a quadratic form ε_n(k) ≈ ε_n(k_0) + (ħ²/2m_n) |k − k_0|² in a small neighborhood of k_0 yields an effective mass m_n that is suitable for describing the low-energy parabolic curvature of that band for use in excitonic reduced-mass calculations, and the exciton reduced mass m_r = m_e m_h/(m_e + m_h) computed from these fitted m_e and m_h is appropriate as input to the effective-BSE provided non-parabolicity and anisotropy are negligible over the momentum region that contributes most to the exciton wave function.

The proposition: For a band energy $E(\mathbf{k})$ computed in Kohn–Sham DFT within the PBE functional, the band effective mass component along a one-dimensional transport direction can be estimated by fitting the band curvature near an extremum (valence-band maximum or conduction-band minimum) using the relation $m^{*}=\hbar^{2}\big/(\partial^{2}E/\partial k^{2})$ evaluated at the extremum, where $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant; the numerical values reported from such fits (for example, $m_{e}^{*}=0.11\,m_{0}$ and $m_{h}^{*}=0.06\,m_{0}$, and $0.02\,m_{0}$ in other cases) are representative of the low-energy carrier inertia provided the fitting window is chosen sufficiently small that the band is approximately quadratic in that window and any band degeneracies are handled correctly, with $m_{0}$ denoting the free-electron mass. The claim excludes effects of quasiparticle renormalization (GW corrections) and non-parabolicity beyond the fitting range.

Define the near-$\Gamma$ expansion of a band energy by $E(\mathbf{k})=E_{0}+E_{2}k^{2}+E_{4}^{(1)}k^{4}+5E_{4}^{(2)}\big(k_{x}^{2}k_{y}^{2}+k_{y}^{2}k_{z}^{2}+k_{z}^{2}k_{x}^{2}-\tfrac{1}{5}k^{4}\big)$, where $k^{2}=k_x^{2}+k_y^{2}+k_z^{2}$ and $E_{2}$ is the quadratic coefficient. Define the effective mass at $\Gamma$ by the small-$k$ relation $E(\mathbf{k})\approx E_{0}+\frac{\hbar^{2}k^{2}}{2m^{*}}$, so that $m^{*}=\hbar^{2}/(2E_{2})$ in the units used. The value $E_{2}=0.754\ \mathrm{Ry\cdot(length)^{-2}}$ determined from the fitted energy surface is sufficiently accurate, given sampling, Fourier fitting, and truncation effects, that the derived effective mass $m^{*}=1.32\,m_{e}$ is a reliable estimate of the band effective mass at $\Gamma$ within the indicated numerical precision.

For DMC-computed discrete single-quasiparticle bands \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) obtained in finite periodic simulation cells for a 2D HEG, define the pathological behaviour as the erratic variation of estimated quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}\\) when the derivative \\(d\\mathcal{E}/dk\\) is estimated by fitting only a very narrow \\(k\\)-range immediately surrounding the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: this near-\\(k_{F}\\) pathological behaviour of \\(m^{*}\\) estimates arises primarily from finite-cell fluctuations (for example, the discrete sampling of \\(\\mathbf{k}\\)-points and cell-induced finite-size correlation effects) together with Hartree–Fock-like non-smooth mean-field features that persist in finite cells, rather than from an intrinsic non-differentiability or singularity of the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle band at \\(k_{F}\\); this explains why expanding the \\(k\\)-range used in the fit reduces the pathology.
